techniques.evidence_quote: the identity-based attack began with social engineering tactics deployed by a state-sponsored threat group, through the popular LinkedIn and WhatsApp platforms. This group posed as professionals seeking technical advice from a select few of the targeted organization's key development staff.

AWS access keys were discovered on the compromised devices as well, giving the attackers two ways into the AWS environment—through direct API access and the web console via compromised Entra ID users.

After convincing these employees to download [what seemed to be harmless] code to their corporate laptops, used regularly for software development, this code harvested the desired access keys and credentials from their devices.
modifying a Lambda function that had the ability to execute code on several previously inaccessible EC2 instances
they discovered an interesting weakness in the victim environment by modifying a Lambda function that had the ability to execute code on several previously inaccessible EC2 instances.
attackers systemically chaining minor permission gaps together to achieve sufficient privilege escalation
authenticated against Entra ID using captured session tokens. This technique not only bypassed multi-factor authentication (MFA), but also circumvented other security controls that were in place.
Each step seemed legitimate within the instance's permissions and was expected in the day-to-day operations of this organization.

The attackers gained access to the organization's Microsoft 365 tenant and authenticated against Entra ID using captured session tokens. This technique not only bypassed multi-factor authentication (MFA).
AWS access keys were discovered on the compromised devices as well, giving the attackers two ways into the AWS environment.
this code harvested the desired access keys and credentials from their devices the attackers were looking for.
leveraging compromised service accounts and SSO trust relationships to move laterally
craft fraudulent API calls that finally gave them access to the critical assets they were seeking

attack_chain.description: State-sponsored threat actors posed as professionals on LinkedIn and WhatsApp, socially engineering key development staff to solicit technical advice and establish trust.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the identity-based attack began with social engineering tactics deployed by a state-sponsored threat group, through the popular LinkedIn and WhatsApp platforms. This group posed as professionals seeking technical advice from a select few of the targeted organization's key development staff.

attack_chain.description: Victims were convinced to download and execute malicious code (infostealer) disguised as harmless content onto their corporate developer laptops.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: After convincing these employees to download [what seemed to be harmless] code to their corporate laptops, used regularly for software development, this code harvested the desired access keys and credentials from their devices.

attack_chain.description: The infostealer harvested AWS access keys, session tokens, and other credentials stored on the developer machines.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: this code harvested the desired access keys and credentials from their devices the attackers were looking for. AWS access keys were discovered on the compromised devices as well.

attack_chain.description: Attackers authenticated to Microsoft 365 and Entra ID using captured session tokens, bypassing MFA and other security controls.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attackers gained access to the organization's Microsoft 365 tenant and authenticated against Entra ID using captured session tokens. This technique not only bypassed multi-factor authentication (MFA), but also circumvented other security controls that were in place.

attack_chain.description: Attackers used stolen AWS access keys to authenticate directly via API and via the AWS web console through compromised Entra ID SSO, gaining dual-path access to the AWS environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: AWS access keys were discovered on the compromised devices as well, giving the attackers two ways into the AWS environment—through direct API access and the web console via compromised Entra ID users.

attack_chain.description: Attackers chained minor IAM permission gaps, leveraging misconfigured IAM policies and SSO trust relationships to escalate privileges and pivot between cloud environments.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: attackers systemically chaining minor permission gaps together to achieve sufficient privilege escalation and in turn leveraging compromised service accounts and SSO trust relationships to move laterally.

attack_chain.description: Attackers modified an existing Lambda function to execute arbitrary code on previously inaccessible EC2 instances, using the Lambda's existing permissions as a privilege escalation path.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they discovered an interesting weakness in the victim environment by modifying a Lambda function that had the ability to execute code on several previously inaccessible EC2 instances. Each step seemed legitimate within the instance's permissions and was expected in the day-to-day operations of this organization.

attack_chain.description: Using the foothold on EC2 instances gained via the modified Lambda, attackers crafted fraudulent API calls to access critical assets within the environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: helping the threat actor gain control over these instances and in turn craft fraudulent API calls that finally gave them access to the critical assets they were seeking.

initial_access_vector: Social engineering via LinkedIn and WhatsApp; victims executed an infostealer that harvested AWS access keys and session tokens from corporate developer laptops
persistence_mechanism: Modification of an existing AWS Lambda function to execute attacker-controlled code on EC2 instances
privilege_escalation: Chained IAM permission gaps and modified a Lambda function with permissions to execute code on EC2 instances, effectively jumping from lower-privilege cloud identities to EC2-level access
